questions.text: A 240 V circuit supplies a component with 20 ohms of resistance. Using Ohm's law, what current flows?

options.t: 4,800 amps (I = E × R)
options.why: That multiplies instead of divides. Ohm's law is I = E ÷ R, so 240 ÷ 20 = 12 A, not 240 × 20.

options.t: 0.083 amps (I = R ÷ E)
options.why: The ratio is inverted. Current is voltage divided by resistance (E ÷ R), not R ÷ E.

options.t: 260 amps (I = E + R)
options.why: Voltage and resistance are not added; I = E ÷ R = 12 A.

questions.why: Ohm's law: I = E ÷ R = 240 V ÷ 20 Ω = 12 A. The electrical section is the largest part of the NATE Core exam, so E=I×R relationships are essential.
questions.ref: NATE Core electrical KATE; basic circuit theory

questions.text: Three resistors are wired in series across a power source. Which statement is true?

options.t: The same current flows through all three, and the individual voltage drops add up to the source voltage
The voltage is the same across each resistor and the currents add
options.why: That describes a PARALLEL circuit. In series, current is constant and voltage drops add.

options.t: Total resistance is less than the smallest individual resistor
options.why: Total resistance dropping below the smallest resistor is a PARALLEL trait. Series resistances add, so total is larger.

options.t: Removing one resistor has no effect on the others
options.why: In series, opening any point breaks the whole circuit — current stops everywhere.

questions.why: In a series circuit the current is the same through every component and the individual voltage drops sum to the source voltage; total resistance is the sum of the resistors. (Parallel is the opposite: shared voltage, currents add.)

questions.text: You need to measure the voltage across a run capacitor and the current draw of the compressor. How do you set up the meter?

options.t: Voltage: meter on volts, probes in parallel across the component; current: clamp (or in series) on amps
Measure both by placing the meter in series on the ohms setting
options.why: Ohms is for de-energized resistance/continuity, never for live voltage or current. Voltage is measured in parallel on volts; current with a clamp/series on amps.

options.t: Voltage in series on the amps jacks; current in parallel on volts
options.why: Reversed. Voltage is measured in parallel (across); current is measured in series or with a clamp on the amps function.

options.t: Use the resistance setting on the live circuit for both readings
options.why: Reading resistance on an energized circuit gives false data and can damage the meter — de-energize for ohms.

questions.why: Measure voltage in parallel (across the component) with the meter on volts; measure current with a clamp meter (or in series) on the amps function. Resistance/continuity is only for de-energized circuits.

questions.text: The sun warms a rooftop unit's cabinet even though no air or contact carries the heat. Which mode of heat transfer is this?

options.t: Radiation — energy transferred by electromagnetic waves, needing no medium
Conduction — heat moving through direct physical contact
options.why: Conduction needs contact between materials. The sun heating a surface across empty space is radiation.

options.t: Convection — heat carried by a moving fluid or air
options.why: Convection requires a moving fluid/air. Solar gain across a vacuum is radiation.

options.t: Latent heat transfer from a phase change
options.why: Latent heat involves a change of state; solar warming of a surface is radiant heat transfer.

questions.why: Radiation transfers heat via electromagnetic waves and needs no medium — how the sun warms a surface across space. Conduction needs contact; convection needs a moving fluid.

questions.text: An air conditioner removes moisture from indoor air without necessarily lowering the thermostat reading during that process. What kind of heat is being removed as it condenses moisture?

options.t: Latent heat — energy removed during a change of state (vapor to liquid) at constant temperature
Sensible heat — because any heat the system removes shows up as a temperature drop
options.why: Sensible heat changes temperature. Removing moisture (condensing water vapor) removes LATENT heat, which changes state without a temperature change.

options.t: Radiant heat — because the coil radiates it away
options.why: Dehumidification is latent-heat removal via condensation on the coil, not radiation.

options.t: No heat is involved in dehumidification
options.why: Condensing water vapor releases latent heat to the coil — dehumidification is very much a heat-transfer process.

questions.why: Latent heat is the energy involved in a change of state at constant temperature; condensing water vapor on the evaporator removes latent heat (dehumidification), distinct from sensible heat, which changes air temperature.

questions.text: Before opening an electrical panel to service a contactor, what is the correct first step?

options.t: De-energize and apply lockout/tagout, then verify zero energy with a meter before touching anything
Start working quickly and just avoid touching the line terminals
options.why: Working "carefully" on a live panel is exactly what LOTO prevents. De-energize, lock out, and verify zero energy first.

options.t: Turn the thermostat down so the unit stops, which makes the panel safe
options.why: A satisfied thermostat does not de-energize the panel — line voltage is still present. Use lockout/tagout at the disconnect.

options.t: Verify with a meter first, then de-energize afterward
options.why: Order matters: de-energize and lock out FIRST, then verify zero energy — not the reverse.

questions.why: The safe sequence is lockout/tagout → verify zero energy with a meter → PPE → work. NATE Core weights safety heavily, and this LOTO sequence is the reliable answer pattern.
questions.ref: NATE Core safety KATE; OSHA LOTO (29 CFR 1910.147)

questions.text: A cooling system's airflow is well below target and the evaporator keeps freezing. Which relationship best explains a dirty filter/coil's effect?

options.t: Added restriction raises external static pressure and lowers airflow (cfm), starving capacity and letting the coil freeze
A dirty filter increases airflow, which overcools and freezes the coil
options.why: Restriction reduces airflow, not increases it. Low airflow — not high — starves the coil and causes freezing.

options.t: Filter condition has no effect on static pressure or airflow
options.why: A dirty filter directly raises static pressure and cuts cfm — a primary cause of low-airflow coil freezing.

options.t: Coil freezing is unrelated to airflow and only about refrigerant charge
options.why: Low charge can freeze a coil too, but low airflow from restriction is a classic, separate cause the airflow section tests.

questions.why: More restriction (dirty filter/coil) raises external static pressure and, for a given blower, lowers airflow. Target is roughly 350–450 cfm per ton; low airflow starves capacity and can freeze the evaporator.
questions.ref: NATE Core airflow/comfort KATE; ACCA Manual D concepts

questions.text: A customer is upset about a repeat no-cool call. Beyond technical skill, what does the NATE Core "soft skills" area emphasize?

options.t: Clear communication, listening to the complaint, explaining the diagnosis, and professional customer interaction
Finishing as fast as possible and leaving without explanation to save time
options.why: Soft-skills competency is about communication and professionalism, not speed at the expense of the customer relationship.

options.t: Soft skills are not part of NATE and never appear on the exam
options.why: The NATE Core explicitly includes soft skills/customer relations as a tested area alongside the technical domains.

options.t: Only the price quote matters; communication is irrelevant
options.why: Communication, listening and clear explanation are exactly what the soft-skills area assesses.

questions.why: NATE Core includes soft skills/customer relations: listening to the complaint, communicating clearly, explaining the diagnosis and behaving professionally — competencies tested alongside safety, tools, electrical and heat transfer.
